articles.title: How Ranveer Allahbadia’s Controversy on ‘India’s Got Latent’ could bring in more stringent OTT regulations
articles.summary: In the Ranveer Allahbadia case, the Supreme Court has signaled its disapproval of the existing digital content and creator ecology. We look closely at the court proceedings that potentially paving the way for the revival of the shelved Broadcasting Bill.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr In the Ranveer Allahbadia case, the Supreme Court has signaled its disapproval of the existing digital content and creator ecology. We look closely at the court proceedings that potentially paving the way for the revival of the shelved Broadcasting Bill. Background In early February 2025, video clips from comedian Samay Raina’s YouTube panel show India’s Got Latent — featuring Ranveer “BeerBiceps” Allahbadia, Ashish Chanchlani, Jaspreet Singh, and Apoorva Mukhija—went viral. Some viewers condemned the jokes, which used explicit sexual references to parents, as vulgar and obscene. The backlash centred on Raina and Allahbadia. On 10 February 2025 the Guwahati Police registered a first‑information report (“FIR”) against the five creators for “promoting obscenity”, citing: (a) Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (“BNS”) Sections 79, 95, 294, 296; (b) Cinematograph Act, 1952 Sections 4, 7; and the (c) Indecent Representation of Women (Prohibition) Act, 1986 Sections 4, 6. The Maharashtra Cyber Department followed suit, booking them under BNS Sections 79, 196, 296, 299 and s. 67 of the Information Technology Act, 2000 at Nodal Cyber Police Station, Thane. Press reports also point to an FIR in Jaipur . Confronted with parallel proceedings in three states, Allahbadia petitioned the Supreme Court (W.P. (Crl.) 85/2025) to quash—or at least consolidate—the Assam and Maharashtra cases. His lawyers mentioned the case before a Bench headed by the Chief Justice of India on 14 February for urgent listing, and the Court scheduled it for hearing on 18 February 2025. Timeline of court proceedings The legal roller‑coaster began on 14 February 2025, when Ranveer Allahbadia hurried to the Supreme Court seeking protection from arrest and a cascade of FIRs registered across the country over an episode of India’s Got Latent . On 18 February 2025, the Bench granted him interim relief but attached four tight conditions: he must cooperate fully with investigators, surrender his passport, remain in India, and halt all new episodes of his show. During the hearing Justice Vikram Nath stated the programme’s language “dirty” and “perverted.” Excerpt from the SC order dated 18.02.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 That same afternoon Ashish Chanchlani, another panellist, secured parallel interim protection from the Gauhati High Court; three days later, the Supreme Court formally tagged his case to Allahbadia’s petition. Excerpt from the Guwahati HC order dated 18.02.2025 in Case No. : AB/359/2025 A semblance of reprieve arrived on 3 March. Acknowledging that the podcast supported roughly 280 jobs, the Supreme Court allowed Allahbadia to restart production—provided he kept the conversation “decent” and steered clear of matters under judicial consideration. Crucially, the Bench asked the Solicitor General to draft proposals for a national regime to regulate online content without trampling free‑speech safeguards. This gave rise to fears of the revival of the Broadcasting Bill which IFF has commented on and analysed extensively. Excerpt from the SC order dated 03.03.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 Procedural hearings followed. On 7 March the Gauhati High Court extended anticipatory bail to Chanchlani, while Allahbadia, on 1 April, filed an affidavit detailing his cooperation with the National Commission for Women and cyber‑police officials. The Solicitor General reported that investigations in Maharashtra and Assam were nearing completion, so the Court kept interim protections alive. The case soon intersected with disability rights. Excerpt from the Guwahati HC order dated 07.03.2025 in Case No. : AB/359/2025 Excerpt from the SC order dated 01.04.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 On 21 April, the State of Maharashtra informed the Court that its investigation was complete and a charge sheet would be filed soon; Assam stated that its investigation was almost complete. Excerpt from the SC order dated 21.04.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 Meanwhile, M/s Cure SMA Foundation sought to join the matter, objecting to Samay Raina’s allegedly ableist jokes. The Court agreed to consider the intervention and asked for a full petition, stressing that creative liberty cannot override the dignity of marginalised communities. Subsequently, matters escalated on the hearing on 28 April. While hearing Cure SMA Foundation’s petition, court observed that the issue of content regulation on OTT platforms falls within the domain of policy-making. The petition had raised concerns regarding the alleged circulation of vulgar and inappropriate material on streaming platforms. While the Court declined to frame regulations itself, citing the principle of separation of powers, it directed the petitioner to serve a copy of the petition to the Central Government for its consideration. On the same date of hearing, the Supreme Court allowed Allahbadia to retrieve his passport but refused to club the Maharashtra and Assam FIRs on that date. Justice Surya Kant reasoned that the Assam complaint alleged distinct offences and its residents should not be forced to litigate in Mumbai. The Bench also chastised police for publicly insinuating Allahbadia’s guilt before filing a chargesheet. That day it instructed Cure SMA to obtain details from Mumbai’s Cyber Cell so every relevant respondents could served and be brought on record. Excerpt from the SC order dated 28.04.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 Finally, on 5 May, the Supreme Court ordered the Mumbai Police Commissioner to serve notices on Samay Raina and four other creators, warning that coercive measures would follow any no‑show. The judges reiterated their intention to frame comprehensive guidelines for online content—a signal that the spirit of the shelved Broadcasting Bill might soon re‑emerge in regulatory form. These proceedings have given a rise to concern and alarm. Excerpt from the SC order dated 05.05.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 The judges reiterated their intention to frame comprehensive guidelines for online content—a signal that the spirit of the shelved Broadcasting Bill might soon re‑emerge in regulatory form. These proceedings have given a rise to concern and alarm. Excerpt from Live Law reporting dated 05.05.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 Proactive Move by MIB: Advisory to OTT Platforms on Content Regulation On 20 February 2025, two days after the Supreme Court lamented a “vacuum in law” on digital speech, the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting (“MIB”) issued an advisory telling over‑the‑top (OTT) services to obey existing rules. Citing a spike in complaints from MPs, regulators and parents about “obscene, pornographic and vulgar” videos, the ministry reminded platforms that the IT Rules 2021 Code of Ethics, the Indecent Representation of Women Act, the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023, the POCSO Act and the Information Technology Act 2000 all criminalise unlawful sexual content. It urged stricter age ratings and warned the two self‑regulatory bodies for OTTs to act against violations. While some may state that the advisory adds no new obligations; it merely re‑states laws that already bind streamers. Even so, the timing—just a week after a parliamentary panel decried “vulgar content” online—suggests the government is preparing the ground for tougher, possibly pre‑screening, controls on digital media. Excerpt from the advisory dated 19.02.2025 issued by MIB While the term "regulation" may sound like an innocuous word, it’s important to recall that the State’s previously proposed legal framework - Broadcasting Services (Regulation) Bill, 2023 (“Broadcasting Bill”), which was so draconian that it faced widespread criticism and was ultimately shelved. The MIB released the Broadcasting Bill 2023 for public consultation on November 10, 2023. The draft bill included “Over-the-Top” (“OTT”) content & digital news published by individuals under the regulatory ambit raising concerns for online free speech and journalistic freedom. Subsequently, in July 2024, another version of the Broadcasting Bill was released but never officially published by MIB, however, several unofficial copies of the 2024 Bill were circulated online. The 2024 Bill reportedly imposed compliance obligations on digital creators impacting their online free speech and creative expression, increased the ambit of persons/ entities regulated under the Bill, retained and introduced ambiguous definitions for significant provisions, and negatively impacted users of online platforms. Read more about the Bill here and here . Last year, amid severe pushback and criticism, the MIB reportedly asked stakeholders to return physical copies of the 2024 Broadcasting Bill, promising to reconsider certain provisions. On August 12, 2024, in a post on X, MIB announced that a revised draft would follow consultations, with the comment deadline extended to October 15. Since then, there has been no update. Despite the MIB’s continued opacity, cosmetic changes won’t fix the Bill’s deep flaws. We demand that the MIB #KillTheBill to protect free speech for digital creators, political commentators, users and other key stakeholders. We have been monitoring these developments and will continue to do so closely. Important Documents IFF’s First Read of the Broadcasting Services (Regulation) Bill, 2023 ( Link ) IFF’s summary of the developments around the Broadcasting Bill since July 2024 ( Link ) SC’s March 3, 2025 order directing the Solicitor General to draft proposals on online content regulation ( Link )

articles.title: IFF’s Statement on EPIC-Aadhaar Linking
articles.summary: Read our statement on EPIC-Aadhaar Linking.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr Is linking your Voter ID with Aadhaar mandatory? Absolutely not, but there’s a caveat! Recent actions by government officials have caused confusion, leading many to fear that they may lose their voting rights if they do not link their Aadhaar to their Voter ID. In this post, we aim to clear up this confusion and present IFF’s statement opposing the integration of Aadhaar with the electoral framework. ECI wants to link Aadhaar to Electoral Rolls On March 18, 2025, the Election Commission of India (“ECI”) released a press note announcing a meeting with the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (“MEITY”), the Unique Identification Authority of India (“UIDAI”), and ECI technical experts, with technical consultations between UIDAI and ECI experts set to commence soon. Press Note released by ECI on March 18, 2025 This development comes amid ongoing concerns over the linking of Electoral Photo Identity Cards (EPIC) with Aadhaar, a process the ECI claims will be governed by Article 326 of the Indian Constitution, Sections 23(4), 23(5), and 23(6) of the Representation of the People Act, 1950, and the Supreme Court's ruling in G Nirajan v. Election Commission of India (WP (C) 177/2023). While the ECI maintains that Aadhaar-EPIC linking is voluntary, its implementation raises serious concerns regarding coercion, voter disenfranchisement, and data privacy. The introduction of Form 6B following the Election Laws (Amendment) Act, 2021 and the Registration of Electors (Amendment) Rules, 2022 made under them exemplifies these concerns, as it presents a false choice: individuals who decline to provide Aadhaar details must explicitly declare they do not possess an Aadhaar number, potentially forcing citizens to make false statements. Section 4 of the Election Laws (Amendment) Act, 2021 inserts three sub-sections to the Representation of the People Act, 1950 . As a result of the Amendment, Section 23(4) of the Act permits electoral registration officers to demand Aadhaar numbers to identify voters. Section 23(5) of the Act states that Aadhaar numbers ought to be provided by individuals in a manner prescribed by rules. Section 23(6) states that inclusion in the electoral roll shall not be denied and entries in the electoral roll shall not be deleted for inability to provide an Aadhaar number due to “such sufficient cause as may be prescribed.” Registration of Electors (Amendment) Rules, 2022 prescribes the “sufficient cause”. Form 6B, introduced by these rules, only permits those who do not already have an Aadhaar to provide an alternate document for verification of their identity. As a result, the Amendments effectively permit the Election Commission to mandate those who have Aadhar Numbers to link them to the electoral rolls despite the fact that previous attempts at Aadhaar-linkage resulted in mass disenfranchisement and an incursion into the fundamental right to privacy. This is reflected in the lack of choice present in Form-6B that forms part of these rules and does not provide any clear field for exempting from Aadhaar updation. Here below field (ii), an alternate ID must be provided only if a person declares, "I don't have Aadhaar number." Hence, there is no option, opt-out or ability for a person not to grant consent if a person does not have an Aadhaar number. Form-6B ECI’s failure to comply with voluntary Aadhaar-EPIC Linking This issue is further underscored by the June 17, 2022 notification from the Ministry of Law and Justice which states that individuals "may intimate" their Aadhaar number, yet Form 6B provides no meaningful opt-out mechanism, creating administrative pressure that effectively transforms this “voluntary” process into a de facto mandatory requirement. Furthermore, the ECI must provide clear and specific information to eliminate any ambiguity regarding the voluntary nature of sharing Aadhaar information, as its press statement on March 18, 2025, did not explicitly clarify this. Read our previously published explainer on how to protect yourself from the coercive linking of Aadhaar with your Voter ID. This has led to a Supreme Court pronouncement on September 18, 2023, where the ECI undertook to the Supreme Court in G Nirajan v. Election Commission of India (WP (C) 177/2023) that, “t he submission of the Adhar number is not mandatory under Rule 26-B of the Registration of Electors (Amendment) Rules 2022 and hence the Election Commission is looking into issuing appropriate clarificatory changes in the forms introduced for that purpose. ” To our knowledge such clarificatory changes to the forms have not yet been carried out. However, the Government of India rejected amendments to Form 6B, claiming an ECI-issued clarification was sufficient—an approach that fails to recognize that placing the burden on voters to justify non-disclosure effectively transforms a supposedly voluntary process into a mandatory one. Hence, at present not only is there a lack of any clear legal basis for making Aadhaar linking mandatory to Voter ID’s but on the contrary the underlying law requires it be voluntary and necessary steps to be taken by the Election Commission to reflect it. We further state that this linking mechanism fundamentally undermines constitutional principles. The right to vote is based on citizenship, to use Aadhaar Numbers, which is not a proof of citizenship , to authenticate the identities of citizens in the electoral rolls. Thus, it is likely that non-citizens may also be placed on the rolls. Even otherwise, the amendments are arbitrary because they assume the Aadhaar Database to be untainted. A number of authorities, including the Comptroller and Auditor General of India , have questioned the sanctity of the Aadhaar Database. Data mismatches between Aadhaar and EPIC databases will lead to arbitrary electoral roll deletions, potentially disenfranchising legitimate voters. Since its inception , the Aadhaar Project has been plagued with instances of mass exclusion , fraud, leakage and biometric failure . Furthermore, the ECI claims over 66 crore individuals have "voluntarily" provided Aadhaar details, yet has not disclosed how this data is being managed or protected, raising serious concerns about consent, potential misuse, and surveillance capabilities. Statement by IFF “The Internet Freedom Foundation opposes the linking of Aadhaar linkage to our electoral framework — directly or indirectly — specifically, as a condition for exercising voting rights. This high technocratic vision is inspired from the magical thinking of voting through smartphones rather than the lived realities of the people of India. This poorly thought out fantasy puts at risk the entire electoral process which already is suffering from questions on its credibility. Going by past experience in the domains of welfare entitlements where Aadhaar has been made mandatory or similar exercises at the state level where it has been linked to voter rolls there is a clear and real danger of mass disenfranchisment, data exploitation and the potential for state survelliance. We urge the ECI and the Government of India to be transparent, follow the spirit of the Supreme Court order and adopt a rights-based approach that prioritizes voter participation without technological barriers. It must eliminate all policies that indirectly mandate Aadhaar disclosure, implement a series of steps towards securing the electoral process as have been recommended by a coalition of campaigns including the Association for Democratic Reforms. This include among many other measures including the non-partisian enforcement and oversight on the social media cells of political parties that are using databases for micro-targetted campaigning in breach of campaign financing limits. The integrity of India's democratic process and our claim of the world’s largest democracy is at stake.” Apar Gupta, Advocate & Founder Director, Internet Freedom Foundation Important Documents ECI’s press note released on March 18, 2025. [ Link ] IFF’s previous explainer on How to protect yourself from coercive Aadhaar-Voter ID linking. [ Link ] Donate Now

articles.title: IFF’s Response to MEITY on the Draft Data Protection Rules
articles.summary: The Draft Data Protection Rules, 2025 weaken privacy rights, expand government surveillance, undermine RTI, and lack accountability, failing to meet constitutional standards for data protection in India.
articles.content: tl;dr The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 is to be operationalised by the Draft Digital Personal Data Protection Rules, 2025 that were put to public consultation ending on March 5, 2025. Our submission to MeitY is premised on a constitutional understanding of data protection highlighting key issues such as vagueness, violations of privacy rights and increasing executive control. Background The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (“the Data Protection Act, 2023”) was enacted on August 11, 2023 following years of deliberation and several iterations. This version was rushed through parliament without any meaningful deliberation after earlier drafts were scrapped . Suffering from excessive vagueness it was reasoned at the time that these details would be operationalised by the delegated legislation in form of rules and regulations. As part of this, the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (“MeitY”) after about 18 months from its passage in parliament released the draft Digital Personal Data Protection Rules, 2025 (“Draft Data Protection Rules, 2025”) on January 3, 2025 for public consultation. Our submission to this consultation outlines the issues with the Draft Data Protection Rules, 2025 from a constitutional perspective. It focuses on data protection as an element within the fundamental right to privacy as per the Supreme Court’s decision in Justice (Retd.) K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India and Others. Justice (Retd.) . Failure to meet Constitutional Privacy Standards Several provisions of the Draft Data Protection Rules, 2025 fail to comply with the Supreme Court’s ruling in the Puttaswamy judgment, which established clear benchmarks for any infringement to privacy and required a data protection law to be made as per a positive obligation of the state to protect it. For instance the statute, nor the rules conform to the proportionality test, and instead grant broad exemptions to government agencies regarding the protection and access to personal data. This results in undermining principles such as purpose limitation and data minimisation. Specifically, provisions such as Rule 22 by granting the Central Government unchecked authority to demand user data from Data Fiduciaries and intermediaries without any judicial oversight, transparency or safeguards creates a parallel framework for state surveillance without any checks and balances. We have presented a tabular view of the problems with Rule 22 for which we have called for a complete withdrawal. Principle Rule 22 under draft Rules Best Practices Judicial Oversight No requirement for court approval before data access A court order or independent review before disclosure User Notification No obligation to inform users when their data is accessed Users must be informed unless exceptions apply (e.g., active criminal investigations) Scope of Government Access Broad and vague terms like “sovereignty, security of the state” without clear reference to Article 19(2). Data access must be justified and proportionate under clear legal frameworks. Ability to Challenge Requests No mechanism for Data Fiduciaries to challenge government demands. Companies and individuals can appeal against overbroad requests. Transparency Requirements No requirement to disclose number or nature of requests. Governments must publish transparency reports on data requests. Undermining RTI and Press Freedoms The right to information, along with the right to privacy is a constitutionally protected right in India. The Data Protection Act, 2023 had already damaged the right to information, which is supposed to co-exist with the right to privacy, by amending the RTI Act. Specifically, the amended Section 8(1)(j) of the RTI Act, 2005 prevents the disclosure of any information that is related to any “personal information”. This upsets a balance, where it previously allowed withholding of personal information if it bore no relation to public activity or interest and thereby constituted an unwarranted invasion of privacy. This change again departs from the proportionality test referenced in Puttaswamy judgment, effectively allowing officials to refuse critical information simply by labeling it “personal.” Here, the deficiencies of the principal law have neither been addressed, nor mitigated by the Draft Data Protection Rules, 2025. Here, we have authored an extensive analysis on these changes and in addition to drawing attention to this in our submissions have joined a broader campaign to #SaveRTI. Expansion of Government Control and Reduced Accountability A law does not enforce by itself and requires an authority or a regulatory body to take charge. Here, the Data Protection Board was in press interactions stated by MeitY to serve as an, “independent authority” overseeing compliance principally through its quasi-judicial powers to impose fines for, “data breaches”. Its powers, while do not include the power to make regulations, do include the ability to summon individuals, examine evidence, and imposing penalties. These powers we foresee will be exercised with compromise and in a partisan manner given its structure and staffing. Rule 16 of the Draft Data Protection Rules, 2025 Rules centralises its appointments, functioning, and decision-making within the executive branch, raising serious concerns about political influence and lack of autonomy​. Since the Data Protection Board is controlled by the executive, this creates risks of bias in adjudication when the state itself is the biggest data fiduciary and processor. We are highlighting some of the problems with this in the table below, greater detail on which is contained within our submissions: Principle Best Practices in Administrative Law Draft Rules Independence of Regulatory Bodies Regulatory bodies must be free from government control, with transparent and independent selection processes. Government-controlled appointments and funding, risking political influence. Judicial Oversight & Review Decisions must be subject to review by higher courts or legislative oversight. No structured appeals process beyond internal government mechanisms. Multi-Stakeholder Representation The appointment process should include representatives from judiciary, civil society, and industry to ensure balance. Search-cum-Selection Committee only includes government officials and experts handpicked by the executive. Transparency and Accountability Regulatory bodies must publish annual reports, transparency records, and disclose decision-making criteria. No mandated transparency requirements or independent reporting obligations. Protection Against Arbitrary Removal Members should have fixed terms and removal only through judicial inquiry. The government retains discretion to dismiss members, creating risks of political interference. Vague and Arbitrary Definitions Allow Misuse The Draft Data Protection Rules, 2025 suffer from significant vagueness creating the possibility for, “pick and choose” enforcement. Poorly defined terms and a lack of clarity on key provisions enable state overreach, opaque corporate practices and inconsistent application. For instance, several critical terms remain vague or entirely undefined, including: "Instrumentalities of the State" – Failure to specify which government-controlled entities are exempt from strict privacy norms, granting excessive discretionary power. "Emergent Situation" – Without a legal or operational definition, this term could justify limitless state access to data without accountability. "Research, Archiving, or Statistical Purposes" – The absence of specific standards allows both – prohibitions on researchers and transparency activists as well as exemptions for companies from seeking user consent by misusing it and labelling commercial work as “research”. "Significant Data Fiduciary (“SDF”)" – The criteria for classifying an entity as an SDF remain unclear, particularly regarding the measurement of data "volume" or "sensitivity." The lack of specificity falls short of acceptable legal standards and best practices as set out in the table below for which we have provided further details in our submissions: Issue Draft Rules Best Practices (GDPR, UK DPA, OECD Guidelines) Clarity of Definitions Vague terms like "instrumentality of the state" and "emergent situation" allow for broad interpretation. Clear definitions for key terms, ensuring precision and preventing abuse. Legal Certainty Ambiguous rules lead to inconsistent enforcement and compliance difficulties. Well-defined obligations for businesses and the state, reducing uncertainty. Government Access to Data State agencies can invoke "national security" without an independent review. Strict necessity and proportionality tests for government data access. User Consent & Rights No clear scope for user challenge if data is collected under exemptions. Users have enforceable rights, including appeal mechanisms against data misuse. Data Processing for Research & Statistical Purposes No limits on how long or how broadly data can be collected under "research" exemptions. Restrictions on secondary data use, ensuring user protection. A data protection law that does not protect Indians The Draft Data Protection Rules, 2025 provided another opportunity for the MeitY to ensure the protection of the privacy of ordinary Indians. While unsurprising, it is indeed disappointing they end up tightening a digital leash while having poorly thought and designed provisions. They mark a continuous failure to comply and meet the constitutional thresholds as set by the Supreme Court on the right to privacy. Through our recommendations, we call for substantial changes to the Draft Data Protection Rules, 2025 in their current form for being poorly considered and increasing the trends towards digital authoritarianism. Important documents Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 [ Link ] Draft Digital Personal Data Protection Rules, 2025 [ Link ] Our submissions to MeitY on the draft Rules [ Link ]

articles.title: YouTube Removes The Wire’s KYC Aadhaar Authentication Video, Sparks Censorship Concerns
articles.summary: YouTube initially banned the Wire video on KYC authentication hardships for violating the platform's policy on "harassment and bullying," but later it only cited "violations of terms of service." The post YouTube Removes The Wire’s KYC Aadhaar Authentication Video, Sparks Censorship Concerns appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: YouTube has removed ( Archived Page ) The Wire’s ground-reporting video on how the Know Your Customer (KYC) process of authentication of a person as per Aadhaar guidelines is depriving people of their fundamental rights, such as education, food, and dignity, resulting in them being caught in vicious bureaucratic circles. The reasons for the removal are still unknown. However, their reporting is still available to view on Facebook . Siddarth Varadaraj, the editor of The Wire, said that the video was removed without prior intimation or notice. “It is unacceptable. We are fighting against it,” he said in a telephonic interview with MediaNama. At the time, we took the screenshot , it read that the “video has been removed for violating YouTube’s policy on harassment and bullying.” However, the URL now shows that the video was removed for “violating the terms of service.” The exact reason for the uninformed removal is not known yet. Can YouTube Remove Videos? YouTube can delete the video from its platform if it has violated the Terms of Service. They are the rules and guidelines for the users of the platform. YouTube claims that the video has been removed because it violates the terms of service. YouTube proactively monitors the violation of its community guidelines , which include child safety, harmful or dangerous content, violent or graphic material, harassment, and spam. To prevent the removal of YouTube videos, users must value others’ privacy and avoid copyrighted content and misleading people, including impersonation and encouragement of illegal activities. Defaming, bullying, harassment, being obscene, or stalking others may also lead to content removal as per the guidelines . Can Governments Order the Removal of YouTube Content? Yes. YouTube had to comply with India’s Information Technology (IT) (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules , 2021. Section 69(A) of the IT Act gives power to the government to block content that threatens “national security, sovereignty, or public order.” This can be sent by either the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) or any other authority. Earlier in 2023, the Union government also mandated that deepfake and misinformation content be removed within 36 hours of reporting. Failing to do so will result in the loss of safe harbour protections under Section 79 of the IT Act. Section 79 protects digital platforms from liability for the content posted by their users. However, the online platform is still liable if it fails to remove unlawful content after the government’s notice/request. To strengthen the content moderation on the internet, the government asked the intermediaries to appoint a chief compliance officer, a nodal contact person, a resident grievance officer, and a physical office in India earlier in 2021. Why It Matters: A 2023 report highlights how 4,000 YouTube URLs were ordered to be removed by the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare. The Indian Cyber Crime Coordination Centre (I4C)– under the Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA) sent 426 content takedown notices to the platform since its inception in 2024. The content takedown notices from both online platforms and the government have been increasing. On the one hand, copyright and privacy are also becoming barriers to freedom of speech ; on the other hand, the government has strengthened its censorship controls through the Sahyog portal. However, this is a developing story. It is not yet clear what the exact reasons are for the removal of The Wire’s reporting. MediaNama is following up with The Wire Team. We will update the copy once we learn the exact reasons for the removal of their video. Also Read: Explained: What is the Sahyog Portal that X called out for censorship? Can The Indian Government Use Section 69 Of IT Act To Censor Digital Media? YouTube Removes India’s Got Latent Episode After Govt Order The post YouTube Removes The Wire’s KYC Aadhaar Authentication Video, Sparks Censorship Concerns appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: 'Court Can't Compel Any State To Adopt National Education Policy' : Supreme Court Dismisses Plea To Implement NEP In Tamil Nadu
articles.summary: Policy news from LiveLaw
articles.content: The Supreme Court on Friday dismissed a Public Interest Litigation filed by a person seeking to direct the State of Tamil Nadu to implement the National Education Policy (2020) including its 'three-language formula'. A bench comprising Justice JB Pardiwala and Justice R Mahadevan observed that the Court cannot issue any direction under Article 32 of the Constitution to compel any State to adopt the NEP. Dismissing the petition filed by one GS Mani, the Court said : " Whether the States should adopt the National Education Policy 2020 or not is a vexed issue. The Supreme Court, through Article 32 of the Constitution, can issue directives to ensure that the rights of the citizens are protected. It cannot directly compel a State to adopt a policy like the National Education Policy 2020. The Court may, however, intervene if a State's action or inaction related to the National Education Policy violates any fundamental rights. We do not propose to examine this issue in this writ petition. We believe that the petitioner has nothing to do with the cause he proposes to espouse. Although he may be from the State of Tamil Nadu, yet on his own admission, he is residing in New Delhi. In such circumstances, this petition stands dismissed." The bench also added that the "main issue may be examined by the Court in an appropriate proceeding." As soon as the matter was taken, the bench asked the petitioner, who was appearing in person, "Who are you? How are you concerned with the National Education Policy?". The petitioner, saying that he is from Tamil Nadu and is now settled in Delhi, said that because of "this kind of policy" (Tamil Nadu schools not teaching Hindi), he could not learn Hindi easily. "Then learn Hindi now in Delhi, na?" Justice Pardiwala told the petitioner. Tamil Nadu Chief Minister MK Stalin recently said that the NEP 2020 will not be implemented in the State, saying that its three-language formula was an attempt to impose Hindi. Case : G.S. MANI vs Government of Tamil Nadu and others | W.P.(C) No. 260/2025

articles.title: Paytm CEO Vijay Shekhar Sharma Fined Rs 1.11 Crore by SEBI Over ESOP Mismanagement
articles.summary: SEBI fines Paytm CEO Rs 1.11 crore, bars him from ESOPs for 3 years over IPO disclosure violations and misuse of influence. The post Paytm CEO Vijay Shekhar Sharma Fined Rs 1.11 Crore by SEBI Over ESOP Mismanagement appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: The Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) has barred Paytm CEO Vijay Shekhar Sharma from accepting fresh Employee Stock Ownership Plans (ESOPs) from any listed company for three years and has imposed a fine of Rs 1.11 crore. SEBI’s directions came as part of a settlement order with Paytm’s parent company, One97 Communications Limited (OCL). What was the case? SEBI issued a show-cause notice to the fintech giant in February 2024, citing the following allegations: Incorrect disclosure: Sharma inaccurately listed himself as a non-promoter of OCL before the company filed its offer documents during the Initial Public Offering (IPO). This was despite him being identified as a promoter in OCL’s FY21 Annual Returns and with no material change in his rights or influence over the company’s management. Greater control over equity: Sharma exercised control over more than 10% equity in OCL, violating SEBI’s 2021 regulations on Share-Based Employee Benefits and Sweat Equity. He allegedly granted himself a large number of ESOPs through a scheme that involved transferring a portion of his equity to a family trust prior to the company’s public debut. Alleged misuse of influence: SEBI also contended that Sharma was in a “position to influence the decision-making” of the Nomination and Remuneration Committee (NRC) while approving ESOPs grants to himself and his brother Ajay Shekhar Sharma. What did SEBI order? In addition to barring Sharma from receiving fresh ESOPs: The regulator also mandated a disgorgement of Rs 35.86 lakh from the sale of 3,720 shares obtained through the exercise of ESOPs. His brother, Ajay Sharma, was directed to pay a settlement amount of Rs 57.11 lakh. SEBI ordered the cancellation of 21 million ESOPs held by Vijay Shekhar Sharma and 222,862 ESOPs held by Ajay Sharma. SEBI formally settled the matter while restricting any further enforcement action against OCL and the Sharma brothers for these violations. Background Allegations of misrepresentation during Paytm’s IPO surfaced in August 2024. In its Draft Red Herring Prospectus (DRHP), the fintech company claimed it was “a professionally managed company and does not have an identifiable promoter.” Under SEBI rules, promoters are ineligible for ESOPs after a company goes public. In response, SEBI also questioned OCL’s board of directors for failing to verify the accuracy of disclosures made in the DRHP. In April 2025, anticipating regulatory action, Vijay Sharma voluntarily surrendered the 21 million ESOPs granted to him. Other scrutiny of Paytm Notably, this isn’t the first time Paytm has faced regulatory action. March 2025 : The Enforcement Directorate (ED) issued a show cause notice to OCL for alleged violation of the Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA), 1999, concerning transactions involving two of its acquired subsidiaries. February 2025 : OCL’s investment and wealth management subsidiary, Paytm Money, paid Rs 45.5 lakh to settle a case involving alleged lapses in meeting SEBI’s technology infrastructure and risk management norms. July 2024 : SEBI warned OCL about non-compliance in related party transactions (RPTs) with the now-defunct Paytm Payments Bank Limited (PPBL). March 2024 : The Financial Intelligence Unit-India (FIU-IND) fined Paytm Payments Bank Limited (PPBL) Rs 5.49 crore for violating provisions of the Prevention of Money Laundering Act (PMLA), 2002. February 2024 : The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) banned PPBL from various activities, including accepting deposits, processing credit transactions, fund transfers, and settling pipeline transactions. Vijay Shekhar Sharma also resigned as the non-executive chairman of PPBL, leading to a reconstitution of the board members. January 2024 : The Indian Highways Management Company (IHMCL) restricted PPBL from issuing FASTags after an audit found non-compliance with service-level agreement (SLA) parameters. March 2022 : PPBL was barred from onboarding new customers after a Rs 1 crore penalty was imposed for allegedly allowing data to flow to servers abroad and inaccurately verifying customers’ identities. Also Read: Paytm Sees Growth in Merchants, But Faces Declining Monthly Users and Profit in Q4FY25 Report: SEBI sends notice to Paytm CEO for misrepresenting facts during listing Vijay Shekhar Sharma resigns from Paytm Bank board The post Paytm CEO Vijay Shekhar Sharma Fined Rs 1.11 Crore by SEBI Over ESOP Mismanagement appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: ‘The Wire’ Website Blocked Across ISPs in India, Publication Cites ‘Blatant Censorship’
articles.summary: The Wire website has been blocked by internet service providers across India, but its accessibility is varying based on ISPs and locations. The post ‘The Wire’ Website Blocked Across ISPs in India, Publication Cites ‘Blatant Censorship’ appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: The website of the independent news publication ‘The Wire’ is no longer accessible across multiple internet service providers in India. In a statement about the website takedown, the publication says that it protests this “blatant censorship” at a critical time and says that it is taking all the steps necessary to challenge this “arbitrary and inexplicable move.” The publication mentions that some Internet Service Providers (ISPs) say that they blocked the site based on an order from the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY), while others state that it was on orders of the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting (MIB). Dear Readers of The Wire In a clear violation of the Constitutional guarantee of freedom of the press, the Government of India has blocked access to https://t.co/mEOYg6zJMu across India. + pic.twitter.com/K1jRk3Vxpy — The Wire (@thewire_in) May 9, 2025 Varying accessibility of The Wire website across ISPs: MediaNama has independently confirmed that The Wire’s site’s accessibility varies across ISPs and locations. For instance, in the national capital region, the site is not accessible on Airtel, Reliance Jio, or ACT. However, only ACT users see the actual notice that the site is inaccessible because of a MeitY order. For others like Airtel and Jio, the ISP just tells users that the site cannot be reached. In other regions, like Goa or Hyderabad, the site is accessible on Airtel and Jio as well. (visible below) As of writing this story, The Wire’s social media accounts ( X , YouTube, Facebook and Instagram ) are still accessible. The publication’s Hindi site is also still accessible. On ACT ISP in Delhi Airtel in Delhi NCR The growing number of content takedowns: The IT Ministry’s action against The Wire comes in the context of a long line of takedowns that the government has ordered since the terrorist attack against Indian tourists who visited the Pahalgam region of Jammu and Kashmir in April this year. While the Wire is the only publication whose site the government has blocked for access in India, other news publications have also been affected by blocking requests on their social media handles. This includes X accounts of independent news organisations Maktoob Media , The Kashmiriyat and Free Press Kashmir . Journalist Sanjay Sharma’s YouTube channel 4PM News is also no longer accessible in India because of national security/public order reasons. Sharma has filed a case challenging the government’s takedown in the Supreme Court. His case also challenges the Information Technology (Procedure and Safeguards For Blocking For Access Of Information By Public) Rules, 2009 . Besides Indian publications, according to a post by X’s Global Government Affairs team, the government has sent takedown requests for over 8000 accounts, including those of international news organisations. Why this matters: Based on The Wire’s statements, it is unclear what specific piece of content led the government to decide to take down the publication’s entire site. This is similar to 4PM News, which, as Sharma mentioned in a statement posted on the news publication’s takedown, said it did not get the opportunity to learn the piece of content that the government had flagged for national security concerns. This lack of transparency around content takedowns has the potential to cause independent news sites to self-censor to avoid losing their platform. Complete site takedowns are not unusual by any means. In February this year , the government gave out orders for the Tamil News website Vikatan after it published a cartoon showing Prime Minister Narendra Modi with his hands and feet cuffed and chained, sitting next to US President Donald Trump. Vikatan took the matter to the Madras High Court, and the court granted the site interim relief, lifting the ban on the site provided the publication took down the cartoon. As Vikatan mentioned in its petition, a complete site shutdown, as the government chose to enforce in all three of these cases, is a disproportionate measure. It can adversely impact the publication in question financially through lost readership, while also depriving citizens of access to journalistic works. Also read: Free speech debate: India orders X to block 8000+ accounts following Pahalgam attack India Crackdown After Pahalgam Attack: Blocks 4PM News, Neha Rathore, Dr Medussa Full List: All the social media accounts the government took down After Pahalgam Attack The post ‘The Wire’ Website Blocked Across ISPs in India, Publication Cites ‘Blatant Censorship’ appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: The Ripple Effects of Trump’s Proposed Tariffs on Foreign Films for Indian Cinema
articles.summary: Trump’s 100% tariff on foreign films may disrupt global cinema, impact OTT platforms, and trigger economic concerns, says IMPPA in letter to PM Modi. The post The Ripple Effects of Trump’s Proposed Tariffs on Foreign Films for Indian Cinema appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: US President Donald Trump had proposed 100% tariffs on all foreign cinema “produced in foreign lands.” We want movies made in America again!” he declared on Truth Social, the social media platform he owns. “I am authorizing the Department of Commerce and the United States Trade Representative to immediately begin the process of instituting a 100% tariff on any movies coming into our country that are produced in foreign lands,” he wrote. He also said that the “messaging and propaganda” films are also a “national security threat.” In response, the Indian Motion Picture Producers Association (IMPPA) sent a letter to Prime Minister Narendra Modi, urging the Indian government to intervene. “This decision can jeopardise the robust cultural and economic ties shared between the two nations in the field of cinema,” read the press release accessed by MediaNama. What is its Impact on OTT? Unlike theatrical releases, movies and web series on OTT platforms don’t have a physical presence. They fall under the “ services ” category rather than “goods.” Therefore, the 100% tariffs are more likely to impact theatrical business than OTT streaming services. A representative from a central OTT platform in India affirmed that the overseas theatrical market for Indian cinema will be more affected than streaming services. He added that OTT platforms don’t produce content in the US—they only license it from Hollywood production houses. However, OTT platforms are not completely insulated from the effects of Trump’s proposed tariffs. Shares of Netflix, Disney, Cinemax, and IMAX dropped by 1.6% to 2% following the announcement, as market uncertainty grew. The Spillover Effects on OTT Business Trump’s broader tariff strategy, including various reciprocal measures , has raised fears of an impending recession. According to a Bloomberg survey of 82 economists, there is a 45% chance of a recession within a year. As per the principle of spillover economics , this could also affect the film and entertainment industry. American streaming services like Netflix and Amazon Prime may reconsider investing in Indian or other international content, as higher tariffs could increase the cost of producing global content. OTT platforms, which operate on both ad-free subscriptions and ad-supported models , may also face revenue dents. Increased tariffs in unrelated sectors like automotive and manufacturing could reduce ad spending. As a result, OTT platforms might raise ad rates, potentially affecting advertisers and consumers alike. Additionally, producers may also increase the price of digital distribution to compensate for the possible costs incurred due to hefty theatrical distribution rates and tariffs. This will disproportionately impact small and independent films. What Counts as a Foreign Film? Trump’s announcement has raised several unresolved questions. White House spokesperson Kush Desai clarified that no final decisions have been made regarding foreign film tariffs. However, the phrase “produced in foreign lands” has led to ambiguities: Does it require a U.S.-based production house or producer, even if the film is shot elsewhere? Is shooting within the US mandatory even for foreign producers? If “foreign land” is the key criterion, what happens when a film is shot in multiple countries? How will the tariff be calculated then? Subsidies Over Penalty “Shooting in the US is bloody expensive,” said Anil Nagrath, Secretary of IMPPA. “It’s self-defeating. It will also kill the producers and the industry,” he added, criticising Trump’s proposed policy. Instead of penalising filmmakers, Nagrath suggested that the US should offer subsidies to both American and Indian filmmakers. Economists often prefer subsidies over tariffs, as they incentivise production without raising prices for consumers. Subsidies allow the state to absorb some of the costs, making it easier for companies to operate without impacting end users . Later the same day, Trump softened his stance, saying he was not “looking to hurt the industry.” “I want to help the industry,” he said . Why it Matters? North America is a key market for Indian cinema—especially for Telugu, Tamil, and Hindi films. Big releases often rely on early overseas premieres to generate buzz and drive domestic marketing campaigns. Since liberalisation, countries like the US, UK, and Canada have become crucial to the success of Indian films. While the proposed tariffs directly impact theatrical distribution, their ripple effects could extend to OTT platforms and ultimately affect consumers. Globally, cinema attendance has already been on the decline . Doubling the cost of foreign film distribution—and thereby ticket prices—may worsen this trend. While this could benefit OTT services in the short term, theatrical performance remains a key metric during licensing negotiations with streaming platforms. Therefore, Trump’s announcement, even if not immediately implemented, should be taken seriously due to its potential long-term policy implications. Also Read: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Unified Media Council Government Focuses on “Clean” OTT Content with ‘Waves’ Launch Supreme Court Rejects Plea for Central Control of OTT Content The post The Ripple Effects of Trump’s Proposed Tariffs on Foreign Films for Indian Cinema appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: Supreme Court Seeks Explanation From MP Sessions Judge For Ignoring Its Bail Order, Asks HC To Send Him For Training
articles.summary: Policy news from LiveLaw
articles.content: The Supreme Court recently frowned upon the conduct of a judicial officer who overlooked the directions passed by the Apex Court in a bail matter. Terming the case to be 'shocking', it issued a show cause notice to the judicial officer as to why no strict view should be taken against him. The Court also requested the Chief Justice of the MP High Court to send the judicial officer, an Additional Sessions Judge, for a week-long training. The bench of Justice Ahsanuddin Amanullah and Justice Prashant Kumar Mishra was hearing a matter related to compliance with its previous bail order, dated January 24, where it granted bail to the accused/petitioner. As per the January 24 order, the petitioner was directed to be released on bail subject to conditions imposed by the Trial Court. When the petitioner approached the Trial Court, the condition set for his release was compliance with the fine awarded, against which the appeal was already admitted and pending. When the petitioner again moved the Supreme Court, the Court on March 7 directed that " In continuation of our dated 24.01.2025, the petitioner shall be released on furnishing bail bonds for a sum of Rs.25,000/- with two sureties of the like amount." The trial court, in its order dated March 8, had (1) sought a copy of the interim application filed by the accused before the Supreme Court to verify if the Trial Court's order dated 17.2.25 was challenged before the Apex Court; (2) It noted that the interim application filed before the Supreme Court by the accused challenged the trial court's 17.02.2025 order concerning whether ₹5 lakh already deposited should be adjusted against the penalty post-conviction. However, the Trial Court observed that the Supreme Court's order dated 24.01.2025 does not explicitly address this issue but continues its earlier direction, allowing release on two sureties of ₹25,000 each. As no clear relief on the penalty deposit was granted, the trial court held that, unless clarified otherwise, the fine remains payable. Nonetheless, given the accused's right to personal liberty and assurance to seek clarification, the trial court directed release upon furnishing two sureties and a personal bond, in addition to the payment of fine. The trial court also asked him to produce within 30 days the order of the Supreme Court or High Court allowing the adjustment of the previously deposited Rs ₹5 lakh or relieving him from requirement to deposit fine. Taking objection to the observations made by the Trial Court, the present bench held that the Trial Court's order " discloses total lack of understanding by the Trial Court and, in fact, amounts to a clear overreach of the orders of this Court. Even the language and tenor of the order is objectionable." Further, it directed the judicial officer to submit a show cause as to why no strict actions should be taken against him. The Chief Justice of the MP High Court was also requested to send the Judicial Officer to a week of training to sensitise him on the need to comply with the Supreme Court's orders.

articles.title: 'Not Court's Duty To Tell Media To Delete' : Supreme Court Sets Aside Delhi HC Order To Remove Wikipedia Page About ANI's Case
articles.summary: Policy news from LiveLaw
articles.content: The Supreme Court on Friday (May 9) set aside the Delhi High Court order , which directed the deletion of a Wikipedia page on defamation proceedings initiated by news agency Asian News International (ANI) against Wikimedia on the ground that the page was prima facie contemptuous and amounted to interference in court proceedings A bench of Justice Abhay S Oka and Justice Ujjal Bhuyan reiterated the principle that Courts are open institutions to the public and the media reporting about judicial proceedings cannot be curbed lightly. Courts as a public institution should always be open to the public, and the issues which are even sub judice can be debated by the public and the press, the bench held. "It is not the duty of the Court to tell the media to delete this and take that down... Both the judiciary and the media are the foundational pillars of democracy, which is a basic feature of the Constitution. For a liberal democracy to thrive, both should supplement each other," Justice Bhuyan read out from the judgment. The judgment relied on the judgment in Sahara India Real Estate Corporation Limited vs. Securities and Exchange Board of India (2012) 10 SCC 603 which held that the Courts can order the postponement of the reporting of judicial proceedings but only if the applicant demonstrates substantial risk of prejudice on the pending trial. It was held in Sahara that the court should pass such an order only when necessary to prevent real and substantial risk to the fairness of the court proceedings. Such a postponement order should be subject to the twin test of necessity and proportionality and will be approrpiate only in cases where the balancing tests otherwise favour the non-publication for a limited period. The postponement order is not a punitive measure but it is a preventive measure, the Court had held in Sahara. The judgment also referred to the 9-judge bench decision in Naresh Shridhar Mirajkar Vs. State of Mahrashtra, where it is held that a "trial held subject to public scrutiny and gaze naturally acts as a check against judicial caprice or vagaries and serves as a powerful instrument for creating confidence of the public in objectivity and impartiality of the administration of Justice." Drawing from these precedents, the Court observed : "The court as a public and open institution must always remain open to public observation, debate and criticism. In fact, courts should welcome debates and constructive criticism. Every important issue needs to be debated by the people and the press, even if the issue of debate is the subject of proceedings before a court. However, those offering criticism should remember that judges cannot respond to such criticism. If a publications scandalizes the court or judges and if a case of contempt is made out, certainly the court should take action. But it is not the duty of the court to tell the media to delete this, take that down. For the improvement of any system and that includes the judiciary, introspection is key. That can only happen if there is a robust debate even on issues which are before the court." The Wikimedia Foundation filed the present petition challenging an order of the Delhi High Court directing the takedown of a Wikipedia page titled “Asian News International v. Wikimedia Foundation”. The High Court took exception to comments on the page, particularly a statement that a judge had threatened to order the shutdown of Wikipedia in India. While issuing notice, the Supreme Court expressed concerns about the High Court's observation that the content amounted to interference with ongoing court proceedings, and the direction to remove material simply because it criticized the High Court. The Supreme Court questioned how the High Court could have passed the order without contempt being proved. Background The dispute began with a defamation suit filed by Asian News International (ANI) against Wikipedia, alleging that content on the Wikipedia page titled “Asian News International” was defamatory regarding its credibility and editorial policies. ANI sought damages of Rs. 2 crores and removal of the material. On November 11, 2024, the Delhi High Court closed ANI's contempt plea regarding the Wikipedia page on the defamatory proceedings after Wikimedia removed the page titled “Asian News International v. Wikimedia Foundation”. Before the Supreme Court, Wikimedia argued that the content was not created by it but by users, and that the relevant statement cited an article published by the Indian Express. It maintained that the page referenced media reports and fell within the scope of open discussion on court proceedings. The Court reserved judgment on April 9, 2025. Subsequently, the Delhi High Court single judge granted interim relief to ANI in the main defamation case and ordered Wikimedia to remove allegedly defamatory content from ANI's Wikipedia page titled “Asian News International.” The High Court division bench upheld the order but partly stayed the single judge's directions, restraining enforcement of the order that directed Wikipedia to remove the protection status of the ANI page and to prevent users and administrators from publishing defamatory content. However, the Supreme Court set aside the injunction orders passed by the Delhi High Court observing that the High Court's direction "to remove all false, misleading and defamatory content" was "very broadly worded" and not capable of being implemented. Senior Advocates Akhil Sibal, Kapil Sibal, Advocates Nikhil Narendran and Tine Abraham appeared for the Wikimedia Foundation. Case no. – SLP(C) No. 7748/2025 Diary No. 2483 / 2025 Citation : 2025 LiveLaw (SC) 550 Case Title – Wikimedia Foundation Inc. v. ANI Media Private Limited

articles.title: Justice Nagarathna Flags Apprehension That Population-Based Delimitation May Reduce Southern States' Representation
articles.summary: Policy news from LiveLaw
articles.content: Justice BV Nagarathna of the Supreme Court on Friday (May 9) orally spoke about the apprehensions that delimitation based on population would reduce the representation of the southern states in the Parliament, since the population growth in the south is declining in contrast to the northern states. The bench comprising Justice Nagarathna and Justice Satish Chandra Sharma was hearing a batch of petitions related to the Surrogacy (Regulation) Act. While dealing with petitions filed by couples seeking a second child through surrogacy, Justice Nagarathna said, "In South you see, the families are shrinking. Births are coming down in South India...There are so many people in the North who are going on and on having children... There is an apprehension now if they have Delimitation based on population, the number of representatives of the South will be reduced because of the population of North India." Justice Nagarathna also questioned why the petitioners wanted to opt for surrogacy. Advocate Mohini Priya for the petitioners, said that they fall under Rule 14 of the Surrogacy (Regulation) Rules, 2022 , which says that a woman can opt for Gestational Surrogacy if the women suffers from medical condition so life threatening, or the intended parents failed to conceive it, or the woman has multiple pregnancy losses etc. She said that both couples tried In-vitro fertilisation (IVF) but it failed. Justice Nagarathna questioned why the couples want to have another child when they already have a biological child who is perfectly healthy. Priya informed that both couples have young girl children and they want to simply extend their family. She added that this is simply in the best interest of the surviving child, and is a matter of personal choice for the couple. " You see, film land celebrities already have two children. That person has two surrogacies and a third child. It should not become a fashion...Everybody wants to be one now. Nobody wants another ," Justice Nagarathna said. To this, Priya replied: " There are few people who still want to expand their family..." The Court also asked if one of the couples had an abortion after having determined the sex of the child to be female. Priya responded that the couple didn't have abortion. Moreover, sex determination is not allowed in law. "Mylords, there was no abortion in this case. She had to terminate the child because there was a life-threatening disorder that she suffered from. We have the doctors recommendation. First child was born in 2016 and for eight years they have been trying another child. There is a genuine medical problem," Priya said. To this, Justice Nagarathna replied: " We are bursting with population literally. One child biologically, second child through surrogacy...In South you see, the families are shrinking. Births are coming down in South India." When Priya replied that a similar thing happened in China and the birth rates decreased so drastically that the Government is promoting a two-child policy, Justice Nagarathna replied that such won't happen in India. "That won't happen in India. There are so many people in the North are going on and going having children... that is an apprehension now if they have Delimitation based on population, the number of representatives of South will be reduced because of the population of North India...If you have one child, why bring in another child. Look at the larger country's interest. So many people, they have decided to not have children. We tell you what the situation is, the in-laws are going for pujas and pilgrimages and the couple have decided not to have children. " Justice Nagarathna said. The Court did not issue notice but tagged it with another writ petition 238/2024, also challenging Rule 4(iii)(c)(II) which disallows having a second child through surrogacy if the first child is perfectly normal. Case Details: W.P.(C) No. 446/2025 and W.P.(C) No. 442/2025

articles.title: Court Finds Google Guilty of Ad Tech Monopoly, DOJ Demands Sale of Key Products
articles.summary: The US DOJ urges Google to sell its ad tech units after being found guilty of monopolizing the digital advertising market. The post Court Finds Google Guilty of Ad Tech Monopoly, DOJ Demands Sale of Key Products appeared first on MEDIANAMA .
articles.content: The US Department of Justice (DOJ) has proposed that Google should sell two of its products in the ad tech space as remedial action against it’s monopoly in the online advertising market, a court filing revealed. The development comes after the Eastern District of Virginia found the search engine developer guilty of “willfully acquiring and maintaining monopoly” in the ad tech market on April 17, 2025. After a three-week bench trial, the US district court concluded that the federal government, along with 17 states, successfully proved that Google commands a monopoly in the open-web display publisher ad server and exchange markets. The court acknowledged that the company had unlawfully tied its DoubleClick for Publishers (DFP), a publisher ad server, and AdX, its ad exchange product, in violation of the Sherman Antitrust Act (It is a U.S. federal law enacted in 1890 to prohibit monopolistic practices and promote fair competition in the marketplace). At the time of delivering the judgment, the court stated it would schedule another hearing to determine appropriate remedies for the company’s antitrust violations. DOJ’s Proposed Remedies: The US DOJ proposed “a mix of structural, behavioral, and administrative remedies,” including the sale of the US-based tech company’s AdX (ad exchange) and the “phased divestiture” or sale of its DFP publisher ad server. To justify the proposal, the department stated that Google leveraged its common control of assets across the ad tech stack to harm consumers and competition in multiple markets. According to the court filing, the department explained that Google integrated its ad tech stack products to work together, enabling the company to carry out its anticompetitive practices. “Specifically, the crux of Google’s campaign was its common control of DFP, AdX, and AdWords, which began with the acquisition of DoubleClick and helped [Google] establish a dominant position on both sides of the ad tech stack,” the DOJ stated in the court filing. “Once Google had DoubleClick’s ad exchange and publisher ad server in hand, it effectively restricted the unique advertising demand offered by AdWords advertisers to AdX.” What did Google say? After being found guilty of violating antitrust law in April 2025, Google stated in a blog post that it disagrees with the court’s verdict and intends to appeal the decision. The company argued that the DOJ’s proposed remedies go beyond the scope of the ruling and could harm consumers, innovation, and the broader tech ecosystem. Google claimed the proposals would make it harder for users to access its services by potentially giving default preference to competitors like Microsoft Bing, and could raise costs for device makers and browsers that rely on revenue from Google search distribution. Additionally, Google expressed concerns over user privacy, stating that the DOJ’s proposals might force it to share sensitive search data with lesser-known companies, increasing potential privacy and security risks. Google’s proposal: In a separate court filing , the company submitted its own proposal for remedial actions. Among other things, Google proposed making real-time bids available to all third-party ad servers for the AdX ad exchange. Additionally, it suggested that its actions be monitored by an independent compliance observer for three years. Background to Google Antitrust Case: According to the procedural history presented in the court filing , on January 24, 2023, the US and eight states filed a complaint against Google for allegedly monopolizing the publisher ad server and ad exchange markets. Later, on February 17, 2023, the company requested that the case be transferred to the Southern District of New York, as 19 civil antitrust actions concerning its ad tech practices had already been moved to that district. However, the court refused the request due to a legal exemption. Why it matters? The US is not the only country where the company has come under fire for its advertising practices. In April 2025, a competition law expert, Or Brook, filed a $6.62 billion (£5 billion) class action lawsuit in the UK against Google for allegedly using its dominance in the internet search space to overcharge other companies for ads and block competition from rival search engines. The lawsuit alleges that Google signed deals with Android phone manufacturers to pre-install two of its products—the Google Search app and Chrome browser. Furthermore, the tech giant allegedly made its ad services work better with its search engine than its rivals’, giving its own services an unfair advantage. Since the US district court has already found the company guilty of breaching antitrust law and is currently deliberating on the appropriate remedies, another case not going in its favor could spell trouble for the search engine developer. Also read: Google’s AI Overviews Hit 1.5 Billion Users as Monetization Grows: Takeaways from Q1 2025 Earnings Call Google’s Gemini AI for Kids: A Learning Tool or a Safety Gamble for India? Earnings Report: Google Cloud Records 141% YoY Increase in Q1FY25 Earnings Amid Rising AI Demand The post Court Finds Google Guilty of Ad Tech Monopoly, DOJ Demands Sale of Key Products appeared first on MEDIANAMA .

articles.title: Unregistered Agreement To Sell Admissible As Evidence To Prove Contract In Specific Performance Suit : Supreme Court
articles.summary: Policy news from LiveLaw
articles.content: The Supreme Court held that an unregistered agreement to sell may be admitted as evidence to establish the existence of a contract in a suit seeking specific performance. The Court said that this arrangement is made possible under the proviso to Section 49 of the Registration Act, 1908, which allows an unregistered document to be received as evidence of a contract in a suit for specific performance or of a collateral transaction. “we are of the opinion that the prayer of the appellant in the interlocutory application falls under proviso to Section 49 of the Registration Act which provides that an unregistered document affecting immovable property may be received as evidence of a contract in a suit for specific performance. The proviso also enables the said document to be received in evidence of a collateral transaction.” , the court said. The bench comprising Justices PS Narasimha and Joymalya Bagchi was hearing an appeal arising from Tamil Nadu, where, by virtue of a State amendment to the Registration Act, it is mandatory to register an agreement to sell. The Appellant while relying on the unregistered agreement to sell (2000) entered with the Respondent, filed a suit for specific performance, asserting that the respondent had accepted part payment and handed over possession of the property, however failed to execute the sale deed. The trial court dismissed the suit, stating that the unregistered agreement to sell was inadmissible under the Stamp Act and Registration Act. Aggrieved by the High Court's affirmation of the trial court's findings, the Appellant moved to the Supreme Court. Setting aside the impugned findings, the Court, relying on the case of S. Kaladevi v. V.R. Somasundara, (2010) 5 SCC 401 observed that an unregistered document can be used to prove an oral agreement of sale in a specific performance suit, not as proof of a completed transfer. Since the Appellant wants to prove the contract formation with the Respondent, and not title, it would be saved by the proviso to Section 49 of the Registration Act, 1908. “When an unregistered sale deed is tendered in evidence, not as evidence of a completed sale, but as proof of an oral agreement of sale, the deed can be received in evidence making an endorsement that it is received only as evidence of an oral agreement of sale under the proviso to Section 49 of 1908 Act” , the court said in S. Kaladevi v. V.R. Somasundara . “It is the case of the appellant that the document sought to be brought on record is intended only to be used as a proof of the oral agreement of sale and that it is permitted under Section 49 of the Registration Act. Under these facts and circumstances, we are of the opinion that the appellant can be permitted to introduce the said document dated 01.01.2000.” , the court held. Accordingly, the appeal was allowed. Case Title: MURUGANANDAM VERSUS MUNIYANDI (DIED) THROUGH LRS. Citation : 2025 LiveLaw (SC) 549

articles.title: Supreme Court Considers Plea To Replace 5-Year Law Degree Course With 4-Year LL.B
articles.summary: Policy news from LiveLaw
articles.content: The Supreme Court on Friday considered a PIL seeking to replace the 5-year LL.B course with a 4-year LL.B course on the ground that the National Education Policy 2020 (NEP 2020) promotes four-year undergraduate courses for professional degrees. A bench comprising Justice Vikram Nath and Justice Sandeep Mehta, did not issue notice on the petition, and tagged it with another matte r concerning 1-year LL.M course. The Public Interest Litigation was filed by Advocate Ashwini Kumar Upadhyay, contending that " 05 years B. Law has been designed for extracting money and the most egregious part is that such dirty trick is being used in the name of education. A five-year course is no benchmark for judging the legal expertise of any student ". The petition also seeks directions to the Centre to set up a Legal Education Commission or Expert Committee consisting of Eminent Educationists, Jurist(s), Retired Judge(s), Advocate(s), and Professors to review the Syllabus, Curriculum and Duration of the LL.B and LL.M Courses and take appropriate steps to attract the best talent in the Legal Profession. The petitioner relies on the New Education Policy, 2020 and states that the policy promotes four-year Graduation Courses in all Professional and Academic Courses but the Bar Council of India (BCI) has not taken appropriate steps to review the existing Syllabus, Curriculum and the Duration of the LL.B and LLM Courses. Today, when Senior Advocate Vikas Singh appeared for the petitioner, Justice Nath asked : "You think something important is raised [in this PIL]?" Singh replied: " I tell your lordship what persuaded me to appear. My Yoga teacher, he is having difficulty in educating his daughter because of the fees structure and he has to pay her fees for five years. Four year, education policy also says. There is another court which is examining whether LLM one year should be banned." Justice Nath suggested that the matter be taken to the Bar Council. Singh then requested that the matter be tagged with the one year LLM matter pending before Justice Surya Kant's bench, which the Court allowed. Earlier, the same petitioner had filed a petition seeking to replace the 5-year LL.B course with 3-year course after Class 12. In April 2024, the Supreme Court refused to entertain that petition, with the then CJI DY Chandrachud commenting, "We need mature people coming into the profession. This 5-year course has been very beneficial." "The Injury Caused to the Students is extremely large because the 05 years duration of BA-LLB and BBA-LLB Course is disproportionate to the Course Material. The Long period puts excessive financial burden on the Middle and Lower-Class family and they are unable to bear such a heavy financial burden. It takes two more years for a student to become the bread earner in his family, " stated the present petition. Case Details: ASHWINI KUMAR UPADHYAY v. UNION OF INDIA AND ORS|W.P.(C) No. 453/2025
